explanation: Whenever prop_0 becomes true (the user stops ventilation), then prop_1 must eventually occur (prop_1 will occur at some future point after prop_0). This is the formalization of a causal effect: 'If stop, then eventually action.'

Whenever the system is in prop_1, a transition to prop_2 (i.e., being in prop_2 in the next step) can only occur if the user has stopped ventilation in prop_0 at the current step. This can be formalized by asserting that if we are at prop_1 and not at prop_0 (indicating ventilation has stopped), then prop_2 will occur next. Alternatively, we globally require that whenever we move from prop_1 to prop_2 in the next step, this only happens if ventilation was stopped in prop_0 now. In LTL: G( (prop_1 & X prop_2) -> !prop_0 )

The transition from PCV to prop_1 should happen if prop_2 holds in prop_0. This is stating that whenever (globally) if prop_2 holds when in prop_0, then at some future point, prop_1 occurs (which we interpret as the transition happening).

Whenever the transition from prop_0 (indicating ventilation is ongoing) to not prop_0 (ventilation stopped) occurs, eventually prop_1 (the new state) must hold. That is, if at some point prop_0 holds and at the next step it does not hold, then eventually prop_1 must become true.

If we are in prop_2 and prop_1 holds in prop_0, then eventually (next) we should transition to prop_3. Since prop_1 'in' prop_0 could be interpreted as 'while prop_0 holds, if prop_1 is true', we use G (globally) to indicate whenever we are in prop_2 and (prop_1 & prop_0), then next (X) prop_3 holds.

Since the only available atomic propositions are prop_0 and prop_1, we cannot refer to system modes or events (like PCV, VentilationOff) directly in the LTL expression. The statement requires us to relate prop_1 in prop_0 with a transition from PCV to VentilationOff, but without mode or transition variables, the only possible LTL constraint concerns the temporal relationship between prop_1 and prop_0. If 'prop_1 in prop_0' means that whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true, then we can express this as 'globally, if prop_1 holds, then prop_0 must also hold.'

The requirement specifies that if the user stops the ventilation in prop_0 (i.e., prop_0 occurs), then prop_1 shall occur. This is a conditional obligation. In LTL, this can be captured as 'whenever prop_0 occurs, eventually prop_1 must occur after that.' This is written as G (prop_0 -> F prop_1).

The specification states that whenever the user stops the ventilation in prop_0, then a transition to prop_1 should happen. This can be represented such that whenever prop_0 becomes false ('the user stops the ventilation in prop_0'), eventually prop_1 will become true. In LTL, this is captured as: whenever not prop_0 becomes true, eventually prop_1 will hold.

Given only the atomic proposition prop_0, we must express the requirement in terms of prop_0 alone. The statement 'The transition from PCV to VentilationOff shall occur if the user prop_0' becomes, in LTL, 'If prop_0 is true, then eventually (something happens)', but with our constraints, the only thing we can say is that if prop_0, then eventually prop_0 (tautological). So with only 'prop_0', the strongest expressible formula is that if prop_0 holds, eventually prop_0 (a tautology, but this is unavoidable with only one atomic proposition).

The transition from PCV (represented by prop_0) to prop_1 should only occur if the user has stopped the ventilation in prop_0. So, whenever prop_1 becomes true immediately after prop_0 becomes false, it should be because the user stopped ventilation (prop_0 became false). In LTL, this can be expressed by stating that if there is a transition from prop_0 being true to prop_1 being true (and prop_0 is now false), this only happens after prop_0 was stopped (became false). We express that 'if at any point prop_1 is true and prop_0 is false, then previously prop_0 was true and has just become false.'
